answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence: The scenarios MUFG New Zealand considered in its scenario analysis are as in Table 9. Scenario: Orderly | Too Little, Too Late | Hot House. Overarching narrative: Collective action against climate change will see a steady and transformative response to limit average temperature to 1.5˚C and achieving net-zero. | Domestic efforts to achieve emissions reductions efforts will be swift, but a delayed global response will lead to New Zealand suffering widespread physical impacts. | Limited progress in policies and technologies, will lead to high physical risks from extreme weather and sea level rise as global warming exceeds 2.5˚C by 2050, which will disrupt social, political, and economic systems.
answers.notes: These are the three overarching narrative scenarios. Each is underpinned by multiple data sources including IPCC SSPs, NIWA RCPs, NGFS, and IEA pathways.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes by 2100 are described: 1.5°C (Orderly scenario), 2.7°C (Too Little, Too Late scenario), and 4.4°C (Hot House scenario). The Hot House scenario also notes global warming exceeds 2.5°C by 2050.
answers.evidence: Global average temperature by 2100: 1.5˚C [Orderly] | 2.7˚C [Too Little, Too Late] | 4.4˚C [Hot House]. Hot House narrative: "global warming exceeds 2.5˚C by 2050".
answers.location_hint: Appendix A: Scenario Analysis, Table 9, p.23
answers.notes: No temperature overshoot language is used; the 2.5°C by 2050 reference in the Hot House narrative is an intermediate milestone, not an overshoot description.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Multiple scenario providers are explicitly named: IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change), NIWA (National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research), Climate Change Commission (New Zealand), NGFS (Network for Greening the Financial System), and IEA (International Energy Agency).
answers.evidence: Climate scenarios and data sources used to underpin each scenario: Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change Shared Socioeconomic Pathways [SSP1-1.9, SSP2-4.5, SSP5-8.5] | NIWA Representative Concentration Pathways [RCP2.6, RCP4.5, RCP8.5] | Climate Change Commissions' Transition Pathway Parameters [Tailwinds, Headwinds, Current Policy Reference] | Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) [Net Zero 2050, Nationally Determined Contributions (NDC's), Current Policies] | IEA Global Energy Pathways [IEA Net Zero Emissions by 2050 (NZE), IEA Announced Pledges Scenario (APS), IEA Stated Policies Scenario (STEPS)]
answers.location_hint: Appendix A: Scenario Analysis, Table 9, p.23
answers.notes: Five distinct providers are named. NIWA is expanded in the glossary as "National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research".

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change Shared Socioeconomic Pathways: SSP1-1.9 | SSP2-4.5 | SSP5-8.5. NIWA Representative Concentration Pathways: RCP2.6 | RCP4.5 | RCP8.5.
answers.location_hint: Appendix A: Scenario Analysis, Table 9, p.23
answers.notes: The document explicitly lists both SSP identifiers (from IPCC) and RCP identifiers (from NIWA) as separate data sources underpinning the same three scenarios. SSPs are from the CMIP6/AR6 framework while RCPs are from the CMIP5/AR5 framework — these are not from the same internally consistent scenario family. The pairings used (e.g. SSP1-1.9 with RCP2.6, SSP2-4.5 with RCP4.5, SSP5-8.5 with RCP8.5) are broadly analogous but are drawn from different generation frameworks.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: The scenarios incorporate New Zealand-specific data sources including "NIWA Representative Concentration Pathways" and "Climate Change Commissions' Transition Pathway Parameters" (Tailwinds, Headwinds, Current Policy Reference), alongside global sources (IPCC SSPs, NGFS, IEA). The "Too Little, Too Late" scenario narrative specifically references New Zealand: "Domestic efforts to achieve emissions reductions efforts will be swift, but a delayed global response will lead to New Zealand suffering widespread physical impacts."
answers.location_hint: Appendix A: Scenario Analysis, Table 9, p.23
answers.notes: The document does not use the explicit words "customised", "downscaled", "adapted", "localised" or "tailored" to describe the scenarios. However, the incorporation of New Zealand-specific data sources (NIWA RCPs and NZ Climate Change Commission pathways) alongside global frameworks, and the NZ-specific narrative framing, strongly implies localisation beyond off-the-shelf use. Confidence is medium rather than high because the explicit customisation language is absent.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.notes: No language such as "participatory", "co-creat", "deliberat", "co-design", "stakeholder input", or "collaborative development" appears in the document in relation to scenario development. The scenarios are drawn from established external providers (IPCC, NIWA, NGFS, IEA, NZ Climate Change Commission).

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: Yes — a "Current Policies" or "Current Policy Reference" scenario functions as a baseline/reference scenario. Under the NGFS framework it is labelled "Current Policies"; under the Climate Change Commission's Transition Pathway Parameters it is labelled "Current Policy Reference"; and under IEA it is labelled "IEA Stated Policies Scenario (STEPS)". These are mapped to the "Hot House" scenario (4.4°C by 2100), representing a no-additional-action reference case against which the Orderly and Too Little, Too Late scenarios are compared.
answers.evidence: Climate Change Commissions' Transition Pathway Parameters: Tailwinds | Headwinds | Current Policy Reference. Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS): Net Zero 2050 | Nationally Determined Contributions (NDC's) | Current Policies. IEA Global Energy Pathways: IEA Net Zero Emissions by 2050 (NZE) | IEA Announced Pledges Scenario (APS) | IEA Stated Policies Scenario (STEPS).
answers.location_hint: Appendix A: Scenario Analysis, Table 9, p.23
answers.notes: The document does not explicitly label any scenario as a "baseline" or "reference scenario" in those exact terms. However, "Current Policies" / "Current Policy Reference" / "STEPS" are standard reference/baseline scenarios in their respective frameworks, and they are mapped to the Hot House scenario. The glossary defines "Baseline" only in the context of GHG emissions targets, not scenario analysis.

answers.question: Model used
answers.answer: No specific climate models or integrated assessment models (IAMs) are named in the document. The scenario data sources referenced are frameworks/organisations (IPCC SSPs, NIWA RCPs, NGFS, IEA, NZ Climate Change Commission), not named models such as CLIMADA, REMIND, MESSAGE, GCAM, etc.
answers.notes: The document lists scenario data sources (IPCC SSPs, NIWA RCPs, NGFS, IEA pathways, NZ CCC pathways) but does not name any underlying climate model or IAM used to generate those scenarios.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: Physical risk terminology: Extreme weather — Acute physical risks, being events of severe climatic events such as floods, hurricanes and fire. Drought — A specific acute physical risk. [...] Physical credit risks for the sectors were all assessed with moderate risk on an inherent basis and a low risk on a residual basis across all three time horizons.
answers.location_hint: Table 2: Physical credit risk heatmap, p.14-15
answers.notes: The scenario analysis explicitly includes acute physical risks (extreme weather events such as floods, hurricanes, fire, and drought) assessed across all sectors in the lending portfolio. Risk ratings (inherent and residual) are assigned. However, quantitative damage/loss estimates in dollar terms are not provided — the assessment uses qualitative "low/moderate/high" ratings.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answer: policy and legal changes (regulations, laws, government policies)
answers.evidence: Physical risk terminology: Extreme weather — Acute physical risks, being events of severe climatic events such as floods, hurricanes and fire. Drought — A specific acute physical risk. Long-term temperature change — A chronic physical risk, being the long-term increase in average global temperatures. [...] Transition risk terminology: Policy and legal — Risks arising from changes in regulations, laws and government policies. Technology — Risks arising from the emergence of new, low emissions technologies. Market — Risks associated with shifts in supply and demand dynamics. Reputation — Risks arising from public perception and stakeholder and investor expectations. [...] Hot House narrative: "high physical risks from extreme weather and sea level rise".
answers.location_hint: Appendix A Table 9 p.23; Tables 2 & 3 pp.14-15
answers.notes: Sea level rise is mentioned in the Hot House scenario narrative. The scenario parameters also include "Technology change response" and "Domestic policy response" as explicit scenario dimensions. Carbon price is not explicitly named as a variable.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: Climate scenarios and data sources used to underpin each scenario: Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change Shared Socioeconomic Pathways [SSP1-1.9, SSP2-4.5, SSP5-8.5] | NIWA Representative Concentration Pathways [RCP2.6, RCP4.5, RCP8.5] | Climate Change Commissions' Transition Pathway Parameters [Tailwinds, Headwinds, Current Policy Reference] | Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) [Net Zero 2050, NDCs, Current Policies] | IEA Global Energy Pathways [NZE, APS, STEPS]. The Too Little, Too Late narrative: "a delayed global response will lead to New Zealand suffering widespread physical impacts."
answers.location_hint: Appendix A: Scenario Analysis, Table 9, p.23
answers.notes: Global-level scenarios (IPCC SSPs, NGFS, IEA) are combined with New Zealand-specific/regional sources (NIWA RCPs, NZ Climate Change Commission pathways). The scenario narratives also reference New Zealand-specific impacts. The document does not explicitly state that global models were "downscaled" to regional level, but the use of NIWA RCPs and NZ CCC pathways implies regional/local application.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Three time horizons are used: Short-term (year end point 2026, ~3 years from 2024); Medium-term (year end point 2030, ~10 years from 2024); Long-term (year end point 2050, ~30 years from 2024). Rationales are provided for each.
answers.evidence: Time horizon: Short-term | Medium-term | Long-term. Year end point: 2026 | 2030 | 2050. Period (relative to 2024): ~3 years | ~10 years | ~30 years. Reasoning: MUFG New Zealand defines its immediate term for its investment portfolio as 3 years, this time horizon allows MUFG New Zealand to stress test active investments and provide a current state assessment. | Aligned with MUFG Group's interim GHG emissions reductions targets. Broadly aligned with average maturity profile of business loans. | Aligned with MUFG Group's long-term GHG emissions reduction targets.
answers.location_hint: Appendix A: Scenario Analysis, Table 10, p.23-24
answers.notes: Justification is explicitly provided for all three time horizons in the "Reasoning" row of Table 10.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.notes: The document lists the scenarios and their data sources in Table 9 (Appendix A) and provides rationales for the time horizons in Table 10, but does not provide an explicit justification for why the specific scenarios (Orderly, Too Little Too Late, Hot House) or the specific data sources (IPCC SSPs, NIWA RCPs, NGFS, IEA, NZ CCC) were chosen over alternatives. No methodology section explains the selection criteria for these particular scenario frameworks.
